rationale_for_baseline_tier: Intractable vomiting with orthostatic hypotension (85/50 standing), tachycardia, dry mucosa and inability to rehydrate orally indicates significant dehydration approaching hypovolaemic shock, and there is urinary retention (palpable bladder) needing decompression — none of which can be assessed or treated via telehealth, and a surgical abdomen cannot be excluded remotely. Urgent same-day in-person/ED assessment for IV fluids, IV antiemesis and bladder catheterisation (T4, with a low threshold to T5/ambulance) is proportionate. T3 is the minimum viable pass. Escalate to T5 for green/bloody vomiting, a rigid abdomen, or collapse.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Collapse/blackout, or worsening faintness with inability to keep any fluids down
escalation_edges.rationale: Indicates hypovolaemic shock needing emergency IV resuscitation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you black out or feel you're about to collapse and can't keep any fluids down, call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Completely unable to pass urine with a painful full bladder
escalation_edges.rationale: Acute retention needs prompt bladder decompression.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't pass urine at all and your bladder feels painfully full, go to ED urgently — you likely need a catheter.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Green/bilious or bloody vomiting, or a rigid board-like abdomen
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you vomit green fluid or blood, or your stomach becomes hard like a board and agonising, call 000 immediately.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone, shocked, unable to rehydrate
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Strong case for ambulance transport; arrange support and monitoring.
Manage the flare and dysautonomia after stabilisation; expect recurrent visceral flares.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Your gut has flared (part of your hEDS) and you're vomiting everything and can't keep fluids down — you're now dehydrated enough that standing nearly makes you collapse, and you can't empty your bladder. This needs hospital care today for a drip, anti-sickness and a catheter. Please arrange to get to hospital now, or call an ambulance if you can't get there safely; lie flat meanwhile.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Attend ED today (ambulance if you can't get there safely); use 000 for the emergency features below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Use ED/000 — this needs same-day assessment regardless of the hour.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note the hEDS visceral flare with orthostatic hypotension (85/50 standing), inability to keep fluids down, urinary retention with a palpable bladder, and the need for IV fluids/antiemesis and catheterisation.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Advising home management/antiemetics/small sips for a shocked, retaining patient, missing hypovolaemic shock, urinary retention or a surgical abdomen.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The patient collapses at home from dehydration, or bladder/renal injury occurs from unrelieved retention.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Immediate ambulance/000 when urgent same-day ED self-presentation (T4) would suffice for a currently supine-stable patient — acceptable and safer than under-triage.
